name: Liminatus Pharma, Inc.

profile.description: Liminatus Pharma, Inc. is a pre-clinical stage biopharmaceutical company developing IBA101, a next-generation CD47-blocking humanized monoclonal antibody for advanced solid cancers including non-small cell lung cancer. The company went public via SPAC merger with Iris Acquisition Corp in April 2025 and began trading on NASDAQ May 1, 2025. Founded in 2018 and based in Cerritos, California with 1 employee. The stock has declined 99.6% from its $33.66 peak to ~$0.12, and management has disclosed substantial doubt about the company's ability to continue as a going concern.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (0/3): Liminatus has a single pre-clinical oncology candidate (IBA101 CD47 antibody) licensed from South Korean company InnoBation Bio in 2023, with CMC services provided by GenScript ProBio [1]. Despite announcing a Phase 1 clinical trial plan in March 2026, the company has not initiated patient dosing. No revenue has been generated since founding in 2018. The company went public via SPAC merger in April 2025 but has not advanced its pipeline meaningfully in the 14 months since. AI Deployment (0/2): No AI products or AI-enabled capabilities. The company's drug development relies on traditional antibody engineering approaches. Market Share (0/2): Liminatus is a single-employee pre-clinical company competing in the CD47 checkpoint inhibitor space where well-funded competitors including Gilead (magrolimab) have scaled back or abandoned similar programs after spending hundreds of millions [1]. The company holds no meaningful market position. Product Pipeline (1/2): IBA101 is described as a 'second-generation' CD47 blockade designed to prevent the severe anemia and thrombocytopenia observed with first-generation approaches [1]. The Phase 1 trial design is a seamless monotherapy dose escalation followed by combination cohorts with PD-1/PD-L1 therapies, with Dr. Se-Hoon Lee as lead investigator. However, the trial has not commenced dosing and successful Phase 1 to Phase 2 progression is anticipated within 2-3 years. The company also intends to in-license CAR-T and companion diagnostic technologies but has no specific assets or timelines. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending of $400K in Q1 2026 out of $1.1M total operating expenses represents 36% of operating costs, above average for companies at this stage [1].

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (1/4): Liminatus describes its purpose as developing novel immune-modulating cancer therapies to 'accelerate much-needed cancer treatments' [2]. Cancer immunotherapy is a noble pursuit, but the mission is generic and interchangeable with hundreds of biotech startups. The company has not demonstrated any unique approach to this mission beyond licensing a single antibody from a Korean lab. Community Impact (0/2): No evidence of charitable programs, community investment, or CSR initiatives was identified for this 1-employee company. Employee Retention (0/2): With only 1 full-time employee (per StockAnalysis.com), there is no meaningful organizational team to evaluate [2]. CEO Chris Kim (appointed November 2022) and CFO Scott Dam are the only named officers. Chief Scientific Officer Dr. Byong-Chul Yoo is a Professor at the National Cancer Center in Korea, suggesting part-time involvement. The governance structure appears inadequate for a public company. Insider Buying (0/2): No insider buying activity was identified. The stock has declined 99.6% from its $33.66 peak (June 2025) to ~$0.12, and no insiders were found purchasing shares at depressed levels. The absence of insider buying at $0.12 per share is notable given that management maintains the drug candidate has value [1].

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Liminatus is a zero-revenue, pre-clinical company with no calculable P/E, PEG, D/E, or ROE metrics [3]. The only available metric — profit margin of 0.0% — scores 0.0 against the 20% target. With only 1 calculable metric scoring 0.0, the weighted average yields Infrastructure 0/10. Liquidity check: average daily volume of 10,096,788 at $0.1192 = $1.20M, barely passing the $1M floor. Q1 2026 net loss of $1.12M against zero revenue. Cash of $1.91M against $1.44M in related-party debt and $223K accrued interest. Accumulated deficit of $40.0M. Total assets of $2.79M with stockholders' equity of just $133K (improved from -$9.8M deficit at year-end 2025 through a $7.36M non-cash Clear Street settlement and $3.44M equity offering in February 2026) [1]. Management has disclosed 'substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern.' Nasdaq compliance notices received for MVLS, MVPHS, and Bid Price Rule deficiencies [1].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (5/9): Drug development is a regulated, capital-intensive, and physically grounded process that AI cannot substitute — clinical trials, FDA approvals, GMP manufacturing, and patient safety monitoring all require human oversight and physical infrastructure that AI structurally cannot replace [1]. However, Liminatus's specific position is more nuanced: the company is so early-stage and resource-constrained (1 employee, $1.9M cash) that AI's impact is largely irrelevant to its near-term survival, which depends entirely on financing and clinical execution. AI-driven drug discovery tools from larger competitors could accelerate competing CD47 programs, potentially eroding whatever scientific advantage IBA101's second-generation design confers. The company is neither meaningfully protected by regulatory moats (it has no approved products) nor directly threatened by AI (there is no revenue to disrupt). AI Opportunity (0/1): No AI-related revenue or AI-enabled capabilities. The company does not use AI in its drug discovery or development process.

infinite_game.summary: Liminatus exhibits strongly finite-game characteristics: a pre-clinical company with existential going-concern risk, 1 employee, Nasdaq delisting warnings across three compliance categories, 99.6% stock price decline, and a $40M accumulated deficit against $1.9M in cash. The company's existence depends entirely on its ability to raise additional capital through continued dilutive offerings. Survival, not purpose, dominates every strategic decision.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): Cancer immunotherapy is a noble pursuit, and the focus on next-generation CD47 blockade that avoids the hematological toxicities of first-generation approaches reflects genuine scientific intentionality [1]. However, with 1 employee, a going-concern qualification, Nasdaq delisting warnings, and failure to advance beyond pre-clinical stage in 8 years since founding, the organizational substance to credibly pursue this cause is virtually nonexistent. The cause exists and is meaningful — developing safer cancer immunotherapies — but it reads more as a licensing wrapper around a Korean lab's antibody than a deeply held purpose that shapes strategic decisions. Capital allocation is survival-driven, not mission-driven.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): With 1 full-time employee and named officers (CEO Chris Kim, CFO Scott Dam, CSO Dr. Yoo at a Korean university), there is no meaningful organizational team to evaluate [2]. The company lacks the scale for team dynamics, psychological safety, or cultural practices. The SPAC merger origin (Iris Acquisition Corp, formerly Tribe Capital Growth I) and multiple Nasdaq compliance failures suggest governance challenges. Shares outstanding nearly doubled in Q1 2026 (17.8M shares issued) through dilutive offerings, further eroding stakeholder trust.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Liminatus operates in a space where Gilead (magrolimab) invested hundreds of millions in CD47 blockade before scaling back, and multiple well-funded pharma companies have struggled with the mechanism's toxicity profile [1]. There is no evidence that Liminatus has strategically learned from these competitors' failures — the company proceeds with a 'second-generation' approach but with minimal resources (1 employee, $1.9M cash) against competitors with thousands of scientists and billions in R&D budgets.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (0/2): The company has not demonstrated strategic flexibility — it has pursued the same single CD47 antibody (IBA101) since licensing it from InnoBation Bio in 2023 without advancing to clinical stage [1]. No pivots, no adjacent programs developed, no strategic adaptation. The announced intention to in-license CAR-T and companion diagnostic technologies remains aspirational with no specific assets or timelines. The company is too small and resource-constrained to pivot meaningfully.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): Pursuing CD47 immunotherapy as a 1-employee company with $1.9M cash when well-funded competitors have struggled with the mechanism requires either extraordinary courage or insufficient awareness of the competitive landscape [1]. The going-concern qualification, Nasdaq compliance failures, and 99.6% stock decline suggest the latter. No evidence of leadership making costly long-term investments or principled decisions — capital allocation is entirely survival-driven.

investment_thesis: Liminatus holds a single pre-clinical oncology asset (IBA101, a next-generation CD47-blocking antibody) in the high-value immuno-oncology space, with a Phase 1 trial announced in March 2026 targeting lung cancer using a seamless monotherapy-to-combination design with PD-1/PD-L1 therapies [1]. The second-generation approach is engineered to avoid the hematological toxicities (anemia, thrombocytopenia) that hampered first-generation CD47 blockades from Gilead and others. At a $5.3M market cap, any clinical validation — even initial safety data — could represent significant upside from a de minimis base. The February 2026 equity offering provided $3.44M in net proceeds, and the $7.36M Clear Street settlement improved the balance sheet. If IBA101 demonstrates a differentiated safety profile in Phase 1, the asset could attract licensing interest from larger pharma partners.
thesis_continued: The overwhelming weight of evidence points to extreme risk across every dimension. The company has 1 employee, zero revenue, a going-concern qualification, accumulated deficit of $40.0M, and cash of only $1.9M against $1.44M in related-party debt [1]. The stock has collapsed 99.6% from its $33.66 SPAC-merger peak to $0.12. Nasdaq has issued deficiency notices for MVLS, MVPHS, and Bid Price Rule violations, with delisting risk imminent if compliance is not regained by mid-2026 deadlines [1]. Shares outstanding nearly doubled in Q1 2026 alone through dilutive issuances (17.8M shares from Clear Street settlement and equity offering). The Phase 1 trial has not yet initiated patient dosing, and successful Phase 1 to Phase 2 progression is anticipated within 2-3 years — during which the company will require substantial additional financing that will further dilute existing shareholders. Major pharma competitors including Gilead have abandoned or scaled back similar CD47 programs after spending exponentially more, raising fundamental questions about the mechanism's viability. No analyst coverage exists for this stock [1][2].
key_catalysts: IBA101 Phase 1 trial initiation and initial safety/pharmacokinetic data could validate the second-generation CD47 approach and attract pharma licensing interest [1]
Regaining Nasdaq compliance through the Bid Price Rule (deadline July 14, 2026) would prevent delisting and preserve access to public capital markets for future financing [1]
Potential in-licensing of CAR-T cell therapeutics or companion diagnostic technologies could diversify the pipeline beyond the single IBA101 asset [1]
key_risks: Imminent Nasdaq delisting risk from three concurrent compliance deficiencies (MVLS, MVPHS, Bid Price Rule) — failure to regain compliance by mid-2026 deadlines would severely impair capital-raising ability [1]

Going-concern qualification with only $1.9M cash against $1.44M related-party debt and $1.1M quarterly operating expenses means the company faces insolvency within 12 months without additional dilutive financing [1]

The CD47 mechanism has a poor clinical track record industry-wide — Gilead invested hundreds of millions in magrolimab before scaling back, and multiple pharma companies have struggled with first-generation hematological toxicities — raising fundamental doubts that a 1-employee company can succeed where billion-dollar competitors failed [1]
